comments.text: I have carefully read [Ko14] and found that equation (4) in [Ko14] seems incorrect, because the equality right above it, namely\[ \frac{1}{2^{3^k} - 2^{3^{k-1}}} = \frac{1}{2^{3^k}} + \frac{1}{2^{3^k} + 2^{3^{k-m}}} + \frac{1}{2^{3^{k+m}} + 2^{3^k}}, \]is false, regardless of whether \(m\) appears in the exponent of 3 or not. I could not find a way to fix this step. However, the result of Elsholtz (arXiv:1606.02117v1) actually covers the bound stated in [Ko14], so the lower bound’s order of magnitude remains correct. Therefore, I suggest that the statement “The lower bound is due to Konyagin [Ko14]” should also cite Elsholtz’s result.

Based on this comment I have sent an e-mail to Sergei Konyagin, and he kindly responded to me. He agreed that the equation you referred to was indeed a mistake. The corrected equation is \begin{align*} \frac{1}{2^{3^k} - 2^{3^{k-1}}} &= \frac{1}{2^{3^k}} + \frac{1}{(2^{2 \times 3^{k-1}}-1)2^{3^k}} \\ &= \frac{1}{2^{3^k}} + \frac{1}{(2^{2 \times 3^{k-1}}-1)(2^{3^k}+2^{3^k-m})} + \frac{1}{(2^{2 \times 3^{k-1}}-1)(2^{3^k}+2^{3^k+m})}. \hspace{10pt} (1) \end{align*}He further explained that he made a few more small mistakes (e.g. the statement of Lemma $1$ in his paper should contain the assumption that $m$ is odd), but the main result still holds. In any case, the point of equation $(1)$ is that the final two denominators are divisible by $2^m + 1$, which by his Lemma $1$ has many divisors, eventually leading to the claimed lower bound on $F(k)$.
